coredata.dc:title: Enhanced detection and absorption of Fe3 + ions using robust fluorescent hydrogels incorporating carbon dots and amphiphilic polyurethane
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

coredata.dc:description: Nanocomposites incorporating carbon dots (CDs) and polymers have gained significant attention for various applications, including heavy metal ion detection. This study presents a novel composite that integrates CDs with amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) for enhanced fluorescence detection and absorption of Fe³ ⁺ ions in wastewater. The resulting nanocomposites exhibit superior mechanical stability, high sensitivity, and selectivity for Fe³ ⁺, with a remarkable 103-fold enhancement in the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe³ ⁺ (from 0.38 µM to 0.98 nM) compared to CDs in solution. This enhancement is attributed to the interaction between the functional groups on the CDs and the polyethylene glycol segments of the PU matrix, leading to a red shift in fluorescence emission and improved stability. Additionally, the composite hydrogels show a 4.2-fold increase in Fe³ ⁺ absorption capacity compared to pure PU, due to the formation of stable complexes between the CDs and Fe³ ⁺ ions. Mechanistic insights into the absorption process are discussed using various modeling approaches. This hybrid fluorescent hydrogel platform presents a portable, cost-effective solution for Fe³ ⁺ detection and absorption in wastewater, with potential applications in bio-imaging, light-emitting diodes, and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrogel
Optical sensors
Ion adsorption
Simulation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-Visible absorption spectra of b-CDs (with inserted images showing fluorescent photographs under 365 nm UV lamp illumination); (b) Fluorescence spectra of b-CDs; (c) UV-Visible absorption spectra of PU-D6 and PU-0.6 %CDs hydrogels; (d) Fluorescence spectra of PU-0.6 %CDs films; and (e) the photograph of fluorescence emission of PU-0.6 %CDs films under different excitation wavelength; (f) the TEM images of b-CDs in solution; (d) the CLSM images of PU-0.6 %CDs film showing the dispersion of CDs in PU; (h) the stretching of the fully swollen PU-0.6 % CDs hydrogel and its stretched knotted sample.
The SEM of the freeze-dried PU hydrogel and TEM images of the PU/CDs hydrogel composite films.

The FTIR spectra of the b-CDs, PU and PU/CDs film: (a) from 450 to 4000 cm−1; (b) the enlarged sections from 1000 to 3000 cm−1; (c) the schematic showing the interactions between the b-CDs and PU chains.

The effects of water contents on the photoluminescent properties of PU-0.6 %CDs hydrogels: (a) the UV-Vis absorbance spectra; (b) the fluorescent emission of the hydrogels under 360 nm excitation; (c) the photos of the hydrogels with and without UV light.

(a) Ion selectivity of PU-0.6 %CDs; (b) calibration curves of the F0/F on concentrations of Fe3+ solution (0.01–200 μM); (c) optical properties of the hydrogels with different metal ions; (d) schematic diagram of quenching mechanism of Fe3+ ion on CDs.

(a) The difference in PL intensity of PU-0.6 %CDs for Fe3+ with different interfering ions (Ca2+, Na+, and Ni2+); (b) the dependence of 1-F/F0 on the concentrations of Fe3+ ions within the range of 0–2.5 nM in lake water.
The dependence of absorption capacity (a) and efficiency (b) of hydrogels on concentrations of Fe3+ ions.
The isothermal absorption modeling of the PU-0.6 %CDs on Fe3+ adsorption: (a) Langmuir isothermal models; (b) Freundlich isothermal models.
(a) The equilibrium adsorption capacity and adsorption efficiency of PU-0.6 %CDs for Fe3+ within different time; (b) Quasi-first-order; (c) quasi-second-order; and (d) Elovich modeling.
The absorption rate calculated from the PFO and PSO modeling.
Isothermal adsorption parameters of Fe3+ by b-CDs/PU-D6/15.
Simulation parameters of PU-0.6 %CDs for Fe3+ adsorption kinetics.

head.ce:title: Enhanced detection and absorption of Fe3 + ions using robust fluorescent hydrogels incorporating carbon dots and amphiphilic polyurethane

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Provincial Key Laboratory of Energy Saving in Phosphorus Chemical Engineering and New Phosphorus Materials, The Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Phosphorus Chemical Engineering of Yunnan Province, Yunnan Technological Innovation Center of Phosphorus Resources, Yunnan International Joint Laboratory of Sustainable Polymers, and Engineering Research Center of Biodegradable Polymers, Educational Commission of Yunnan Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocomposites incorporating carbon dots (CDs) and polymers have gained significant attention for various applications, including heavy metal ion detection. This study presents a novel composite that integrates CDs with amphiphilic polyurethane (PU) for enhanced fluorescence detection and absorption of Fe³ ⁺ ions in wastewater. The resulting nanocomposites exhibit superior mechanical stability, high sensitivity, and selectivity for Fe³ ⁺, with a remarkable 103-fold enhancement in the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe³ ⁺ (from 0.38 µM to 0.98 nM) compared to CDs in solution. This enhancement is attributed to the interaction between the functional groups on the CDs and the polyethylene glycol segments of the PU matrix, leading to a red shift in fluorescence emission and improved stability. Additionally, the composite hydrogels show a 4.2-fold increase in Fe³ ⁺ absorption capacity compared to pure PU, due to the formation of stable complexes between the CDs and Fe³ ⁺ ions. Mechanistic insights into the absorption process are discussed using various modeling approaches. This hybrid fluorescent hydrogel platform presents a portable, cost-effective solution for Fe³ ⁺ detection and absorption in wastewater, with potential applications in bio-imaging, light-emitting diodes, and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The sensitive detection and effective removal of heavy metal ions from wastewater are critical for maintaining environmental quality and human health. Various techniques have been developed for the removal of heavy metal ions, including chemical precipitation, ion exchange, adsorption, and membrane filtration [1,2]. However, there is still a growing need for novel materials and technologies that can efficiently detect and remove heavy metal ions from wastewater with high sensitivity, selectivity, and cost-effectiveness. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention for environmental pollution control [3] due to their unique photoluminescent (PL) properties, excellent aqueous stability, abundance functional groups, and absorption capacity [4–6]. Statistically, sensor application of CDs is the most frequently reported for its ultra-high sensitivity [5]. CDs form a smart system capable of emitting or quenching fluorescent signals in the presence of specific analytes, including heavy metal ions [7,8]. For example, the detection of Cu2+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 6 nM [9] and LOD of 0.05 µM for Fe3+ have been reported [10]. However, designing a portable, robust, and adsorptive CDs-based sensing platform for an operational, on-site, quick, and accurate sensing remains challenging [11], since most of the reported CDs are used in solution states and the possible aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching in the solid states.

Embedding CDs in hydrogel is a win-win situation, combining the high sensitivity of CDs for detection with the high swelling capacity of the hydrogel matrix [12–14], without causing CDs agglomeration. Hydrogel efficiently removes heavy metal ions from wastewater through adsorption or chelation mechanisms due to its large surface area [15,16], however, without detection sensitive. The fluorescence emitted by the CDs makes up for this by providing a real-time, visual indication of heavy metal ion presence, enabling sensitive detection at low concentrations [17]. Additionally, CDs endow hydrogels with novel properties, such as film separation abilities due to chelation with heavy metal ions [11] and enhanced mechanical properties [18,19]. By harnessing the unique properties of CDs and hydrogels, fluorescent hydrogels are prepared, offering cost-effective and environmentally friendly solutions for monitoring and mitigating heavy metal ion contamination.

Different CDs-based fluorescent hydrogels for heavy metal ion removal have been developed [20–22]. For instance, Guo et al. synthesized a poly(acrylamide) (PAAm)/cellulose nanofibrils/CDs hydrogel for heavy metal detection and absorption [23]. The hydrogel showed a low LOD for Fe³ ⁺ (18 mg/L) and high absorption capacity for various heavy metal ions (769, 212, 2056, and 1246 mg/g for Fe³⁺, Ba²⁺, Pb²⁺, and Cu²⁺, respectively) due to the cellulose nanofibrils skeleton [23]. In another study, Li et al. fabricated a cellulose-based/CDs hydrogel, exhibiting good sensitivity for Hg(II) detection and extraction in water, with a LOD of 3.0 × 10⁻⁶ M and an absorption capacity of over 662.25 mg/g at room temperature [24]. Recently, spherical hydrogel particles using chitosan, gelatin, and N-CDs have also been reported for heavy metal ion removal [25]. Additionally, other carbon-dot hydrogels have been reported for removing perfluorooctane sulfonate from water [26] and for VOC absorption using aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIE) based hydrogels [27].

Despite these advancements, fluorescent hydrogels with CDs are still in their infancy. Constructing a portable CDs based hydrogel with robust mechanical properties and high absorption capacity is critical for scaling up to industrial applications. However, several challenges are remained: (1) To maximum the performance of CDs in polymer composites, solving the problems of CDs aggregation and uniformity is critical [20]; on the other hand, further research about the effects of CDs on the properties of polymer composites is needed; (2) the relationship between the structure and properties of the CDs-based absorbent is still uncertain. For example, an in-depth understanding of the adsorption mechanism would help to reveal the nature and law of the adsorption process of CDs-based adsorbent and promote further development in the field of environmental adsorption [3].

A suitable polymer matrix, which possess high mechanical performance and Herein, we prepared a robust CDs based hydrogel using shape-memory polyurethane with adjustable mechanical properties as the hydrogel matrix [28]. The fluorescent hydrogels exhibited adjustable mechanical and photoluminescent properties, allowing for sensitive detection and effective removal of Fe³ ⁺ ions from wastewater due to the specific coordination between Fe³ ⁺ and the CDs. Meanwhile, Fe3+ ions are of particular concern due to their widespread presence and detrimental effects on both human health and the environment. Excessive Fe3+ ions can lead to various health issues, including organ damage and neurological disorders, while also contributing to environmental problems such as water pollution. Remarkably, the hydrogel reported here leverages the synergistic properties of CDs and polyurethane to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity in Fe3+ ion detection while providing robust mechanical performance. Our approach not only demonstrates the potential of these hydrogels in environmental monitoring but also opens new possibilities for their application in diverse fields requiring efficient metal ion sensing and absorption.

The O-phenylenediamine (AR grade) and wheat bran used for CDs preparation, as well as the ions for ion detection (including FeCl3·6 H2O, CrCl3·6 H2O, SnCl2, CdCl2, NiCl2·6 H2O, BaCl2, ZnCl2, and MnCl2 in AR grade) were purchased from Aladdin, Shanghai, China. The HydroMed D6 (AdvanSource Biomaterials Corporation, USA; referred to as PU hereafter) used for hydrogel preparation is a typical amphiphilic polyurethane with polyethylene glycol (PEG) as crystalline hydrophilic segment. All materials were used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was produced with a Millipore Milli-Q Plus ultra-pure water system (TKA-GenPure).

O-phenylenediamine and wheat bran in a mass ratio of 4:1, were dissolved in DI water, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 3–4 using hydrochloric acid. The mixed solution was stirred thoroughly and transferred to a water-jacketed high-pressure reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). The reactor was heated in a high-temperature oven at 120 ℃ for 6 h. Upon completion of the reaction and cooling, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 30 minutes to remove large insoluble particles. The supernatant obtained after centrifugation was filtered using a sand core filtration device and a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, the filtrate was transferred to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da and dialyzed for 24 h to remove unreacted small molecular impurities. Finally, the solution collected from the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain a powder of blue fluorescent carbon dots (b-CDs).

The quantum yield (QY) of the prepared b-CDs was measured by relative method. Quinine sulfate is dissolved in 0.05 M sulfuric acid solution, and its QY is 55 % when excited at 350 nm. It can be used as a standard for measuring QY by Eq. (1).
(1)
φ
x
=
φ
s
∙
F
x
F
s
∙
A
s
A
x
∙
η
x
η
s
Where,
φ
s
and
φ
x
represents the QY of standards and b-CDs. F, A, η represent fluorescence intensity, absorbance, and refractive index of the solvents at excitation wavelength respectively. F is calculated from the area S of the fluorescence emission peak in the range of 480–620 nm.

The PU solution in a mixture of ethanol and deionized water (9:1) was prepared according to our previous research [28]. Different amounts of b-CDs (0.4 wt% (10 mg), 0.6 wt% (15 mg), and 0.8 wt% (20 mg) based on the neat PU-D6 weight were added into the PU-D6 solutions and stirred at room temperature for 2 hr. The resulting mixed solution was poured into PTFE dishes and dried in a vacuum oven at 40 ℃ for 24 h. Dried PU/CDs films were obtained, designated as PU-0.4 %CDs, PU-0.6 %CDs, and PU-0.8 %CDs, respectively. The water contents of PU/CDs films were regulated by an infrared lamp oven the equilibrium swelled gels (the water content of the gels was 89 % at equilibrium swelling state). In summary, different fluorescent materials, including CDs in DI water (named as b-CDs), solid-stated dry PU/CDs (named as PU/CDs film) and swelled PU/CDs with different water contents (named as PU/CDs hydrogel) were obtained.

The PL properties of the synthesized b-CDs, PU/CDs film and hydrogels were characterized by Ultraviolet-Visible absorption (UV-Vis) spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, Shimadzu UV-2250, Japan) and fluorescence emission spectrophotometer (RF-6000 PC, Shimadzu, Japan)) at intervals of 1 nm within the range of 200 ∼ 600 nm. For PL properties testing, as least 3 repeated samples were tested.

The FTIR spectra of b-CDs and the PU/CDs film was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (NICOLET 5700, US) in the spectral range 4000–400 cm−1 with the resolution of 4 cm−1.

The morphological of the b-CDs were captured by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan)). The sample was collected via dropping the CDs on a copper mesh with ultra-thin carbon film. To check the dispersion of b-CDs in the PU film, the PU/CDs film was observed by a laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM).

The swelled PU hydrogels and its PU/CDs composite hydrogel were fractured in liquid nitrogen and then freeze-dried at - 40 C for 24 h by using freeze dryer (ALPHA 1–2LDplus (Martin Christ GmbH), Fisher) for the scanning electron microscope SEM (SU-8220, Hitachi, Japan) observation, all the dried samples were coated with gold. For TEM (JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan) observation, the PU/CDs composite film were embedded in epoxy resin for ultra-thin specimens cutting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and absorption of heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The ion detection of the PU/CDs composites for various ions were conducted as follows: the dry PU/CDs composites film strip was dipped in 500 μM metal ion solution (Ba2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+) for 2 min, followed by taking out the strip to measure its fluorescence intensity (F). The baseline fluorescence intensity (F0) was determined by measuring the fluorescence after dipping in water for 5 min. To further investigate the sensitivity of CDs optical detection of Fe3+, a series of Fe3+ solutions with concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 200 μM were prepared. The same procedure was applied to test the fluorescence intensity.

Meantime, the detection of the Fe3+ by the pure b-CDs solution were also characterized. 20 mL of b-CDs was diluted into 100 mL using deionized water to create a standard solution. Subsequently, 1 mL of the standard solution was extracted and combined with 2 mL Fe3+ solutions ranging from 0.01 to 200 μM, the mixture was allowed to stand for 2 h for fluorescence intensity testing.

The fluorescence intensity was obtained by integrating the fluorescence curves within the range of 300–500 nm. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated based on triple standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity in the absence of metal ions.

The absorption of the PU and PU/CDs hydrogel for Fe3+ were carried out by dipping the PU and PU/CDs film in 20 mL of 500 mg/L Fe3+ solution for 12 h to reach the adsorption equilibrium. The concentration of Fe3+ in the solution before and after absorption was measured by UV–visible spectrophotometer, thus the adsorption capacity of hydrogel was calculated by the Eq. (2):
(2)
q =
c
0
c
×
V
m
Where the c0 and c are the concentration of Fe3+ solution before and after adsorption, respectively, the V is the volume of solution, and m is the dry weight of adsorbent (hydrogel).

The tensile properties of the pure PU film and swelled hydrogels were characterized by a universal testing machine (ETM105DP with a 500 N load cell, Shenzhen Wance Testing Machine Equipment Co., Ltd.) at a crosshead speed of 50 mm/min, following the standard GB/T1040. For each sample, five parallel specimens were tested at 25 °C, and the average values were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of fluorescent nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: To determine whether embedding b-CDs in a PU matrix affects the optical properties of CDs, UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectroscopy of the CDs and their composite films are presented in Fig. 1. The b-CDs exhibited typical absorption and fluorescence emission behavior with a quantum yield of 7.04 %, as shown in Figs. 1 a and 1 b. Three absorption peaks corresponding to the π-π* transitions of conjugated CC bonds (233 nm), n-π* transitions of CO (269 nm), and the n-π* transition peak of C-N bonds (447 nm) was observed for the b-CDs. Additionally, the fluorescence emission of the b-CDs shifted to longer wavelengths with increasing excitation wavelengths, exhibiting a typical red-shift phenomenon. Meanwhile, the morphology of the b-CDs characterized by HRTEM (Fig. 1 f), revealed a diameter of around 2 nm, indicating the successful synthesis of the b-CDs.

In comparison, the pure PU-D6 did not exhibit any absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region (Fig. 1 c), indicating the absence of fluorescent groups in the PU molecules. With the introduction of b-CDs, the PU/CDs film exhibited prominent absorption peaks at 257 nm, 283 nm, and 418 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 c. Meantime, it was found that increasing the b-CDs content enhanced the intensity of the film but did not significantly change the fluorescence absorption and emission wavelengths, as shown in Fig. S1.

However, the absorption peaks of b-CDs in the PU matrix were altered compared to the b-CDs solution. The π-π* transitions of CC and n-π* transitions of CO shifted to higher wavelengths, while the n-π* transition peak of C-N bonds shifted to lower wavelengths. The longer absorption wavelength indicates that the energy gap (ΔE) between the bonding and anti-bonding orbitals is decreased, requiring less energetic (longer wavelength) photons for excitation. This may be attributed to strong interactions between the b-CDs and PU matrix. The interaction between the functional groups on the surface of b-CDs and the PU chains generates new energy levels, thereby reducing the energy of electron transitions and causing the red shift in fluorescence emission. The enhanced fluorescent intensity and stability of the nanocomposites in contrast to the fluorescent properties of CDs solution has also been reported previously [29].

The fluorescence emission of PU/CDs films depended on the excitation wavelength, exhibiting a red-shift with increasing excitation wavelengths from 320 to 380 nm, as showed by the photoluminescent (PL) spectra in Fig. 1 d. Compared to the fluorescence emission of b-CDs (Fig. 1 g), the red shift of the CDs in the PU matrix was more pronounced. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission intensity of the composites was also much higher than that of the b-CDs, leading to a color transition from blue to green under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1 e). Although the b-CDs in solution also exhibited longer emission wavelengths at longer excitation wavelengths, i.e., > 400 nm, the PL intensity was quite low, as shown in Fig. 1 b. Therefore, the pure CDs in solution did not exhibit an obvious color change under different excitation wavelengths.

The dispersion state of the CDs in PU films was characterized by TEM and fluorescence microscopy. The scattered fluorescent spots in Fig. 1 g demonstrated the homogeneous dispersion of the CDs in the PU matrix. The PU hydrogels exhibited typical porous honeycomb-like structures after water removal as showed in Fig. 2. The addition of 0.6 % CDs had minimal impact on the morphology of the PU hydrogel, likely due to the low content and small size of the CDs. The dispersion of CDs within the PU film was further observed using TEM (Fig. 2). The results revealed that the CDs were homogeneously dispersed within the PU matrix without significant agglomeration, consistent with the observations from fluorescence microscopy.

The fluorescent hydrogels also exhibited excellent mechanical performance. The strong and toughened swollen PU/CDs hydrogels with 89 % water content can be easily knotted and stretched by hand, as shown in Fig. 1 h. This resulted from the excellent inherent mechanical properties of the PU itself, with the tensile strength of the PU film (0 wt% H2O) and swollen PU hydrogels (89 wt% H2O) reaching 28.9 and 2.6 MPa, while the tensile strains were 873 % and 516 %, respectively (Table S1), as reported by us previously [28]. The introduction of such a low amount of b-CDs (maximum 0.6 wt%) in this study did not affect the mechanical performance of the hydrogels.

The interaction between b-CDs and the PU was proved by the FTIR spectra in
Fig. 3. The chemical structure of the PU has been reported in our previous study, which is consist of hydrophilic PEG and hydrophobic segments [28]. As reported, the triplet peaks around 1127 cm−1 were ascribed to the ʋ(C-O-C) of the PEG chains while the peaks at 2850 and 2920 cm−1 was corresponding to the ʋ(CH2) absorption. With the introduction of b-CDs, both the triplet peaks and ʋ(CH2) absorption peaks changed to board and flat peaks, as showed by the Fig. 3 b. The disappeared peaks at 1127 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 in b-CDs were resulted from the stretching vibration of C-O-C and -CH functional groups on the surface of CDs respectively [30]. Therefore, it is advised that the interaction happened mainly between the b-CDs and the hydrophilic PEG segments in PU chains, as illustrated in Fig. 3 c. This is not surprising since the CDs were previously reported to be highly hydrophilic [31].

Resulting from the excellent water absorption capacity of the PU, fluorescent hydrogels with different water contents were prepared. The swelling behavior of the PU was not significantly influenced by the introduction of b-CDs, with an equilibrium water contents of 89 % (Fig. S2). The UV-Vis absorption and PL spectroscopy of the hydrogels with different water contents were showed in Fig. 4. Both the UV-Vis absorption and PL intensity of the hydrogels decreased with increasing water contents. The decreased intensity was not surprised. Firstly, the transition from crystallization to amorphous of PU with increasing water contents [28] allows more light transmission; secondly, the dispersion of b-CDs in the matrix has been changed from dense to sparse dispersion with water contents increased. This effectively reduced the concentration of CDs in PU, ultimately leading to the intensity reduction in PU/CDs hydrogels as evidenced by the photos in Fig. 4 c.

Furthermore, no leaching of CDs from the PU matrix was observed, even after reaching equilibrium swelling. The PL intensity of the water solution after swelling the PU/CDs showed no signs of a fluorescent phenomenon. However, the dried films from the equilibrium-swelled fluorescent hydrogel exhibited strong fluorescent emission again. The UV-Vis absorption and PL intensity were identical to the films made by solvent casting, and the intensity was much stronger than that of the PU/CDs solutions, as shown in Fig. S3. These results indicate that the confinement of CDs in the matrix not only prevented fluorescence quenching but also enhanced the fluorescence intensity of CDs, similar to the phenomenon of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications – detection and absorption of heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the CDs can be used as fluorescence probes for different metal ions detection [3,33]. To explore whether the embedding of b-CDs in PU would influence its detection ability, various different metal ions including Ba2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ were selected to study the fluorescence detection capacity of the composite films. The composites showed high selective for Fe3+. Via dipping the PU/CDs film into the Fe3+solutions for 2 min, a significant quenching of fluorescence intensity was observed, as showed in Fig. 5 a and c. The sensitivity of the CDs to Fe3+ but not to other ions is likely due to the Fe3+ exhibits strong electron attraction andincomplete d-orbita [34], which could form stronger interaction with the functional groups (-COOH, -OH, -NH2, etc.) on the surface of CDs, leading to the formation of a stable complex, which has also been observed in previous studies [10,23,35].

The selectivity for ion detection originated from the b-CDs itself. The ion detection capacity of b-CDs in solution was evaluated, with results provided in Fig. S4. It was found that the prepared b-CDs exhibited unique sensitivity to Fe3+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.38 µM. Interestingly, embedding the b-CDs in the PU matrix greatly decreased the LOD from 0.38 µM to 0.98 nM, a 103-fold improvement. The LOD was determined by plotting the ratio of fluorescence intensity before and after quenching (F0/F) against the log (concentration of Fe3+ ions) from 0.01–200 μM, as shown in Fig. 5 b. The non-linear relationship, with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.996, predicted a relatively low detection limit of 0.98 nM for Fe3+ ions in water at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. This LOD was much lower than that of the CDs in solutions, such as the Syzygium cumini-based CDs (0.001 µM) [35], green bean based CDs (60 nM) [36], carboxymethylcellulose based CDs (0.14 µM) [37], alginic acid/ ethanediamine based CDs (10.98 µM) [38], and comparable to the fluorescent hydrogel based on QDs-doped hydrogel fiber (14 nM) [39], PEG diacrylate (PEGDA)/ CDs hydrogel (Hg 2+ for 4 nM) [40], and agarose/CDs hydrogel (0.5 nM) [11]. A comparative study of the LOD in our research with others were summarize in Table S2 in supporting information. It can be found that the LOD of the CDs is reduced after it incorporated into the hydrogel matrix, either in our study or from previous researches. The lower LOD may be due to the restricted movement of Fe3+ ions within the PU hydrogel network, which facilitates their binding to b-CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching and a lower LOD. This suggests that the LOD of CDs can be effectively enhanced by the restriction provided by a polymer network.

We also evaluated the Fe³ ⁺ detection performance in the presence of 200-fold excess of interfering ions (Ca²⁺, Na⁺, and Ni²⁺), as shown in Fig. 6 a. The results indicate that Ca²⁺ and Na⁺ ions only cause minimal interference with the fluorescence intensity (F/F₀) of Fe³ ⁺. However, the presence of Ni²⁺ ions significantly increases the fluorescence intensity, with the PL intensity of the PU-0.6 %CDs in the Fe³ ⁺/Ni²⁺ solution being identical to that of DI water without any ions. This suggests that high concentrations of Ni²⁺ ions severely interfere with Fe³ ⁺ detection, leading to the failure detection of Fe³ ⁺ in solutions. This interference is likely due to the selective adsorption properties of the fluorescent hydrogel, which preferentially adsorbs Ni²⁺ ions over Fe³ ⁺ ions. As shown in the ion selectivity data, Ni²⁺ ion adsorption does not directly affect the fluorescence intensity, but it occupies the adsorption sites on the carbon dots, weakening the adsorption of Fe³ ⁺ and lowering the sensor's detection limit.

To assess the sensor’s performance in real-world conditions, we tested it with lake water samples from LaoYu Lake in Kunming, Yunnan, China. After filtering through a 0.22 μm membrane and centrifuging at 12,000 rpm for 20 min as reported by Liu et al. [41]., they were spiked with Fe³ ⁺ at varying concentrations. As shown in Fig. 6 b, the PL intensity decreased with increasing Fe³ ⁺ concentration from 0.5 to 2.5 nM. Despite potential interference from minerals and organic matter in the lake water, the sensing platform successfully distinguished between fresh lake water and water spiked with 2.5 nM Fe³ ⁺, meeting the sensitivity threshold for Fe³ ⁺ detection in drinking water (20 μM) as set by the US EPA. These results suggest that, with further development, this robust, mechanically stable sensor could be effectively used for Fe³ ⁺ detection in potable water.

Fluorescence quenching can be mainly divided into dynamic quenching and static quenching, while understanding the fluorescence quenching mechanisms is crucial for optimizing the performance of fluorescent materials and designing novel fluorescent sensors or probes. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ by CDs were evaluated here. Normally, the dynamic quenching can be described by the dynamic quenching Stern-Volmer Eq. (3) [42]:
(3)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
where the [Q] is the concentration of the quencher Fe3+, K
SV is the dynamic quenching rate constant. For dynamic quenching, the K
SV is rather low with a value of ∼ 10−10, this is because of the static quenching is typically a relatively slow process as it involves the formation of chemical bonds or complex between the quencher and CDs.

By fitting the fluorescence intensity of CDs solutions containing different concentrations of Fe3+, it was found that the K
SV = 253.19. This value is much greater than the maximum value of the dynamic quenching rate constant (∼10−10), hence it can be inferred that the quenching of CDs by Fe3+ belongs to static quenching, where Fe3+ forms a complex with CDs leading to fluorescence quenching (Fig. 4 d). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels with 500 μM Fe3+ at different temperatures from 25 to 45 ℃, and the quenching is typically temperature-independent with almost identical F0/F as showed in the Fig. S5. It further indicates the static quenching mechanism of the Fe3+ with fluorescent hydrogel.

In addition to the increased LOD of b-CDs, the absorption capacity of the PU hydrogels has also been significantly enhanced by the addition of b-CDs, as shown in Fig. 7. Due to the lack of interactions between pure PU and heavy metal ions, PU without CDs exhibited a poor absorption capacity for Fe3+, with a value of approximately 40 mg/g. However, the absorption capacity and efficiency of the hydrogels significantly increased to 207.3 mg/g and 51.8 %, respectively, with the introduction of only 0.6 wt% (15 mg) b-CDs. This enhancement results from the complex formed between the functional groups (OH or -NH2) on the surface of b-CDs and Fe3+ ions. However, further increasing the CDs content to 0.8 wt% decreased the absorption capacity and efficiency, likely due to the agglomeration of b-CDs, which reduced the contact opportunities between -OH or -NH2 groups and Fe3+ ions. The absorption capacity also depended on the concentration of the Fe3+ ion solution, increasing linearly with higher concentrations of Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 7 b.

To thoroughly investigate the interaction mechanism between the PU/CDs hydrogels and the Fe3+, we conducted a detailed analysis of the isothermal absorption of the hydrogels using the classic Langmuir and Freundlich models. The modeling results were presented in Fig. 8. The correlation coefficient (R2) of the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models were higher than 0.99, indicating that the equilibrium data for the adsorption of Fe3+ into PU/CDs hydrogels can be well represented by these two models but best fitted with Langmuir isotherm model. The Langmuir model, which assumes uniform adsorption sites and monolayer adsorption, showed a higher correlation coefficient of 0.9995 (Fig. 8 a and Table 1), indicating it accurately describes the Fe3+ adsorption on PU-0.6 %CDs hydrogel, suggesting a monolayer adsorption mechanism. In contrast, the Freundlich model, suitable for multilayer adsorption on heterogeneous surfaces, exhibited a lower correlation coefficient (Fig. 8 b).

The adsorption kinetics of the hydrogels were also studied and simulated with different adsorption kinetic models; the results were given in Fig. 9. As showed in Fig. 9 a, the adsorption of Fe3+ in 20 mL of 400 mg/L Fe3+ solution by PU-0.6 %CDs reached equilibrium in approximately 100 min, the maximum adsorption capacity was 207.6 mg/g as measured after 12 h absorption. Since Langmuir models can better describe the isothermal adsorption behavior of the hydrogels, three improved nonlinear fitting method developed by Wang and Guo [43], based on the traditional Pseudo-first-order (PFO) kinetic model (Eq. 4), Pseudo-second-order (PSO) kinetic model (Eq. 5) and Elovich equation (Eq. 6) were used to simulate the above equilibrium absorption behavior.
(4)
q
t
=
q
e
( 1 − EXP ( −
k
1
t ) )
(5)
q
t
=
q
e
2
k
2
t / ( 1 +
q
e
k
2
t )
(6)
q
t
=
1
b
ln
1 + abt
Where the qe (mg/g) and qt (mg/g) are the equilibrium adsorption capacity and adsorption capacity at any time, respectively, k1 (min−1) and k2 (g/mg· min−1) are the equilibrium rate parameters for PFO and PSO, respectively, t is the time, a and b are constants. The above models were used to fit the absorption experimental results of the PU-0.6 %CDs hydrogels. The fitting curves were presented in Fig. 9(b-d) and the results were given in Table 2.

The fitting results showed that the PFO model was more favorable to describe the adsorption kinetics of the hydrogels, showing a high R2 of 0.9987. The R2 of PSO model was only 0.7066, indicating its low correlation. The PSO kinetic models are often used to describe processes involving chemisorption, where the rate of adsorption is controlled by the chemisorption mechanism. PFO kinetic models are often used to describe physical adsorption processes, which assume that the adsorption rate is proportional to the number of unoccupied adsorption sites. Since the correlation coefficient of the PFO kinetic model is closer to 1, it can be inferred that the adsorption process of Fe3+ ions by PU/CDs hydrogel is mainly dominated by physical adsorption. On the other hand, the small value of k2 obtained also indicated that the chemisorption is rather slow. However, the calculated absorption capacity values (qe,cal) from the PSO model is more closer to the experimental data (qe,exp) compare to the qe,cal from the PFO model. It is hypothesized that the deviation of the PSO model could be resulted from the rather high initial concentrations of the Fe3+ (C0 = 400 mg/L). The decreased correlation coefficient of the PSO fitting resulting from the increased C0 of the absorbate has also been reported previously [44].

Further more, we simply calculate the PFO and PSO rates based on the Eqs. (7) and (8) [43]:
(7) PFO rate = k1 * (qe-qt)
(8) PSO rate = k1 * (qe-qt)2

The absorption rate of different models was presented in Fig. 10. It is interesting to find that in the initial stage of adsorption (within 20 min), the rate of PSO is faster than that of PFO. However, as time goes on, PFO adsorption gradually exceeds PSO adsorption. The results indicate that the adsorption of hydrogels containing b-CDs is a complex process, encompassing both physical and chemical adsorption. Initially, the functional groups on the surface of b-CDs interact with metal ions [9], forming coordination complex or through ion bonding or electron transfer [33], which primarily involves chemical adsorption. As the available sites on the surface of b-CDs become occupied, metal ions are subsequently adsorbed onto the surface of b-CDs or within the hydrogel through physical adsorption, eventually reaching an equilibrium state.

The Elovich modeling also showed high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9957). As an empirical equation without definite physical meanings, the Elovich model has been successfully used for modeling the adsorption of metals ions and organic pollutants on adsorbents for a long time [45,46]. Based on the basic assumptions of the Elovich model, it is predicted that the surface of the adsorbent, i.e., CDs should be heterogeneous [43], and the interaction between the CDs and Fe3+ was responsible for the absorption of the metal ions.

In this study, we successfully developed an amphiphilic polyurethane hydrogel embedded with carbon dots (CDs), offering a highly effective platform for both fluorescence detection and absorption of Fe³ ⁺ ions. The incorporation of CDs into the PU matrix resulted in an extraordinary enhancement of the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe³ ⁺, achieving a 103-fold improvement from 0.38 µM to 0.98 nM. This enhancement is attributed to the interactions between the functional groups on the CDs and the PU matrix, which alter the energy gap between electron orbitals, leading to stronger fluorescence. Moreover, the composite hydrogel exhibited a significantly higher absorption capacity for Fe³ ⁺ ions compared to pure PU, making it an effective absorbent for heavy metals in wastewater. Kinetic modeling further confirmed that the enhanced absorption is driven by the complex formation between CDs and Fe³ ⁺ ions. These findings position this hybrid hydrogel as a promising material for portable, cost-effective Fe³ ⁺ detection and remediation. Beyond heavy metal detection, the hybrid hydrogel also holds potential for applications in bio-imaging, fluorescent printing, and anti-counterfeiting, representing a significant advancement in fluorescent sensor technology. Future work will explore these additional applications to fully realize the versatility of this material.

Liu Yu-xin: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Wu Feng: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Li Tianxiang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Zhang Nengcui: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Lan Xu-ting: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Xie Yu-Hui: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Feng Dong: Methodology, Conceptualization. Xie Delong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mei Yi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
